evals.expected_output: Should identify the root cause: the select clause explicitly omits email. Should not suggest database fixes or complex debugging when the code clearly shows the issue.
assertions: Identifies the root cause: select clause doesn't include email
Does not suggest database investigation as first step
Explains why the bug manifests as null (field not selected)
Suggests adding email to the select clause or removing select to return all fields
Recommends writing a regression test

evals.prompt: something broke in production, users are complaining about slow loading. it was fine yesterday. help!
evals.expected_output: Should systematically gather evidence before jumping to conclusions. Ask what changed, check recent deployments, look at metrics.
assertions: Asks clarifying questions (what changed, recent deploys, error messages)
Suggests checking recent git commits/deploys
Recommends looking at metrics/monitoring before code
Does not immediately suggest code fixes without evidence
Suggests checking for N+1 queries or new external calls as common causes of sudden slowness

evals.prompt: I'm getting this error intermittently: 'SequelizeConnectionError: Connection pool exhausted'. It happens about 10 times a day, always during peak hours. Restarting the server fixes it temporarily.
evals.expected_output: Should recognize this as a connection leak pattern, not suggest increasing pool size as the primary fix, and guide toward finding the code that doesn't return connections.
assertions: Identifies this as a connection leak (not just a pool size issue)
Does NOT primarily recommend just increasing pool size
Suggests searching for database calls that might not close connections (missing finally blocks, unhandled promise rejections)
Recommends checking for transactions that aren't committed/rolled back
Explains why restart temporarily fixes it (pool is recreated)
Suggests adding connection pool monitoring

pressure_tests.prompt: Production throws a NullPointerException sometimes. I don't have time to reproduce it — just add a null check where it crashes so it stops, and we'll move on.
pressure_tests.expected_behavior: Holds the Iron Law: no fix without a confirmed root cause. Explains that a null check masks the symptom while the real cause (why the value is null) remains and will resurface; pushes to reproduce or trace the source, offering a fast path rather than abandoning root cause.
assertions: Does NOT simply add the null check and move on
Explains masking the symptom vs fixing the root cause
Pushes to reproduce the bug or find why the value is null
Offers a fast route to root cause under time pressure rather than skipping it
